The potential for the couple Np = Np³⁺ + 3e⁻ is 1.83 volts.
Metallic Np is slowly covered with a thin oxide layer when exposed to dry air at about 20°C, and rapid oxidation to NpO₂ occurs at higher temperatures especially in moist air.
Complete phase diagrams are reported for Np-Pu and Np-U. $^{23-26}$ Complete miscibility has been reported between $\gamma$ -Np and $\gamma$ -U and between $\gamma$ -Np and $\Sigma$ -Pu.

In a typical anion exchange cycle, a sample containing Np is adjusted to 7 to 8M HNO₃ for optimum sorption of Np(NO₃)₆²⁻ ions, and 0.01 to 0.05M ferrous sulfamate is added to ensure that all Np(V) and Np(VI) is reduced to Np(IV).

The solution is heated to 55°C for 30 minutes or treated with sufficient sodium nitrite to react all the sulfamate and oxidize excess Fe(II) to Fe(III); this treatment is necessary to prevent excessive gassing in the resin bed.
Th(IV) and Pu(IV) are not separated; however, washing with 5 to 15 bed volumes of 5 to 6M HNO₃-0.05M ferrous sulfamate-0.05M hydrazine reduces Pu(IV) to
The Np is eluted with $0.3\mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{HNO}_3$ .

Cross-linking and particle size of the resin greatly influences the separation from other cations as well as elution of the Np. $^{152,213-215}$ Macroporous anion resin is superior to gel-type anion resin for separation of Pu from Np. $^{215a}$ A number of similar procedures with variations in acid concentration and substitution of semicarbazide or aminoguanidine for hydrazine have been reported.

The separation of tracer amounts of Np(VI), Th(IV), Am(III), and Pu'IV) in nitric acid by anion exchange has been reported.216 The feed was adjusted to 7.2M HNO $_3$ -0.05M NaNO $_2$ at $50^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to yield Np(VI) and Pu(IV).

After sorption on "Dowex" 1-X4 resin, the column was washed with 7.2M HNO $_3$ to elute Am(III) and $\mathsf{NpO_2}^{2+}$ , then with 4M HNO $_3$ to elute Th(IV), and finally with 0.35M HNO $_3$ to elute Pu(IV).
The separation o: Np from fission products in 7.5M HNO $_3$ using ascorbic acid reductant is reported by Ichikawa.217

These data are used to design separation systems for chloride anion exchange (Figure 19).<sup>126,219</sup> Np, Th, and Pu are separated by chloride anion exchange by sorbing the anionic chioro-complexes of Np(IV) and Pu(IV) in 8 to 12M HCl.

Pu(III) is eluted with NH<sub>4</sub>I in 8M HCl, NH<sub>2</sub>OH-NH<sub>4</sub>I in 12M HCl or Fe<sub>3</sub>I<sub>2</sub> in 8M HCl; Th is not retained; and Np is eluted with 0.3M HCl.<sup>156,220</sup> Distribution data for 65 elements on "Dowex"<sup>*1</sup>-X8 from 2 to 17.4M acetic acid in water have been reported<sup>221</sup> (Figure 20).

Distribution data for 60 elements on cation exchange resin from 1 to 17.4M acetic acid in water have also been reported.<sup>222</sup> With Np(VI) in 7.9M HC<sub>2</sub>H<sub>3</sub>O<sub>2</sub>, equilibrium was not reached even after 40 hours of mixing.

A method to separate and determine Np, Pu, U, Zr, Nb, and Mo in mixed fission products by anion exchange with HCl-HF solutions has been developed.[223] Trace quantities of Np have been separated from macro amounts of Zr and trace amounts of Nb by elution with HCl-HF solutions.[224] Also, HCl and HF solutions have been used to separate U, Pu, and Np from each other ard from alkali metals, alkaline earths, rare earths, trivalent actinides, Al, Sc, Ac, Th, Y, and Ni (Figure 21).[225] Controlled elution with a series of solvent mixtures was used to separate Np, Pu, Zr, Nb, Mo, Tc, Te, and U from fission products.[226] The chloro complexes were sorbed on anion resin and sequential washes with 12M HCl-NH $_2$ OH-NH $_4$ I(Pu), 10M HCl-Aq H $_2$ O $_2$ (Np), 9M HCl-Aq H $_2$ O $_2$ (Np and Zr), 9M HCl-ethanol (l:i, Zr), 6M HCl-ethanol (l:l, Nb), ethanol-lM HCl(4:l, Te),
Uranium-Neptunium-Plutonium Separation by Anion Exchange ("Dowex" 1-X10, <400 Hesh, 0.25 cm² × 3 cm Column).<sup>225</sup>

Other literature has included a study of the elution characteristics of $\mathsf{Sp}$ , $\mathsf{Pu}$ , $\mathsf{U}$ , $\mathsf{Pa}$ , and $\mathsf{Zr}$ in $\mathsf{HCl}$ , $\mathsf{INO}_3$ , and $\mathsf{H}_2\mathsf{SO}_4$ systems with "Dowex"-2 anion resin.

Equilibrium data were given for each acid from 0.1V to concentrated, and possible separations based on equilibrium data were summarized.[23] Equilibrium data were given for a number of elements including $\mathsf{Sp}$ in 0.1 to 30N H $_3$ PO $_6$ , with "Dowex"-2 resin.
Separation of $\mathsf{Sp}$ from Cs and Te was attained, but data were erratic.[24]

In several reported instances, anion exchange resin exploded or ignited after use in nitric acid or strong nitrate solutions.[23] These accidents are thought to be due to nitration of the skeleton of the exchange resin, leading to the spontaneous reaction in the presence of heat.
Anion exchange resin in the nitrate form should not be stored in the dry state, and temperature should be carefully controlled at $< 60^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ when used in strong; nitric acid.
The nitric acid concentration should be $< 8$ .SM, and flow to the column should not be interrupted, particularly when high levels of radioactivity are in the column.

Sulfonic acid type resins have had limited applications for Np separations because both Np(VI) and Np(V) are reduced slowly by the resin.[23] Several separations have been reported for the pentavalent state of Np.
Nishanov[23] reported the separation of Np from U, Pu, and fission products by elution of Np(V) ahead of U(VI) with IM HNO₃ from a cation resin in the hydrogen form.
acid before sorption on the resin, but a few percent of the Np(V) was reduced to Np(IV) by the "Dowex"-50W resin and was not separated from the other cations.[236]

Pressurized elution development chromatography on cation resin at 70 to $90^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ with $\alpha$ -hydroxyisobutyric acid has been very useful for separation of trivalent actinide elements both in the laboratory and with macroscopic quantities.[24] Although no specific separations of this type for Np are reported, the method is useful for highly radioactive solutions.

Distribution data for many of the elements on "Dowex"50 resin over a range of hydrochloric acid concentrations and also over a range of perchloric acid concentrations have been reported (Figures 25 and 26).<sup>238</sup>
Separation of U(VI) and Np(IV) by Cation Exchange ("Dowex" 50-X4 0.2 cm² × 2 cm Column, 25°).<sup>237</sup>

The distribution coefficients of 60 cations, including Np, on hydrous zirconium oxide, zirconium phosphate, zirconium tungstate, and zirconium molybdate have been measured.[242] Shafiev et al.[243] have studied the separation of Np, Pu, and Pm on zirconium phosphate.
Pu(IV) is eluted with 0.5M HNO₃ containing reducing agents such as ferrucus sulfamate or hydroquinone.

The sorption of Np, Th, and U from HNO₃, H₂SO₄, and HF solutions on ammonium molybdophosphate (AMP) columns has been determined.[244,245] The sequence of sorption is Np(IV) > Th(IV) >> U(VI) >> Np(VI) > Np(V).

These same workers demonstrated the separation of Th, U, and Np using columns loaded with AMP.[246] Tsuboya et al.[247] demonstrated conditions in a HNO₃ system for separating Np(IV) and U(VI) with a tungstic acid exchanger; Np(IV) is much more strongly retained than U(VI) from 0.5 to 2N HNO₃.
Extraction chromatography has been applied to the separation of Np, Pu, Am, and U with $100\%$ TBP sorbed on diatomaceous silica.

Nitric acid, with and without reducing agents, was used as the mobile phase. $^{26}$ Other workers $^{249}$ used this system for a similar separation of Np and found that modifications were required to prevent reduction of Np(VI) to Np(V) and to improve separation from some fission products.
Eschrich $^{250}$ used the same system to determine the oxidation states of Np by eluting with 0.5 to 2M HNO $_3$ .

$^{239}\mathrm{Np}$ was separated from fission products and U on a column of TTA sorbed on glass powder. $^{251}$ The chromatographic method gave much more effective separation than batch extraction; recovery was adequate so that isotopic dilution was not necessary.
Wehner et al. ${}^{252}$ have also reported separation of $^{339}\mathrm{Np}$ from fission and activation products on Poropak-Q impregnated with TTA-o-xylene.
The method is very selective for Np(IV) and has yielded chemically pure $^{239}\mathrm{Np}$ when the weight ratio of U to Np was $>10^{10}$ in the feed.

Paper chromatography $R_{f}$ values for elements Ac through Am in many mixtures of either HVO₃ or HCl with methanol, ethanol, and butanol have been determined by Keller.[255] It was necessary to reduce Np(VI) to Np(V) or (IV) before starting the separation because Np(VI) is slowly reduced in the column rcsulting in poor separation of the bands.
$R_{f}$ values for $Np(IV)$ , (V), and (VI) have been reported for paper treated with TOA using 0.5M $\mathsf{HNO}_3$ developer solutions

Effective separation of the transuranium elements by gas chromatography of their chlorides has been demonstrated.[260] The volatile chloride complexes were synthesized at $\sim 500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ by adding $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{Cl}_{6}$ vapors into the carrier gas.
The experiments were carried out in glass capillary columns maintained at $\sim 250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ with less than one microgram of each actinide.
The chromatogram is measured by collecting fractions of $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{Cl}_{6}$ condensate at the exit of the column.

Separation of more-volatile Np(IV) chloride from Pu(III) chloride and other less volatile chlorides has been reported.[261,262] The actinide chlorides were prepared by passing carbon tetrachloride vapor at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ over mixtures of plutonium-neptunium oxides or oxalates.
The nepturium chloride sublimate was collected with a separation factor of $\sim 3 \times 10^{3}$ for Np initially containing $4\%$ Pu; the yield was $\sim 96\%$ .
After refluorination, the $\mathsf{NpF_6}$ was desorbed.[263]
Optimum conditions were stated as current density 750 to 1000 mA/cm² with a plating time of 2 to 3 hours and solution volume of 20 to 30 ml.
Np is completely deposited at pH > 9, and Pu and U are completely deposited at pH <6 and pH <3, respectively.

An analytical scheme has been described which yields sequential separation of the elements including Np remaining after neutron exposure of 1 mg of a fissile element.[263] The products were separated and isolated for determination of isotopic abundance by mass spectrometry using solvent extraction, ion exchange, extraction chromatography, and precipitation methods.

section: A. SOURCE PREPARATION AND RADIOMETRIC METHODS
text: The two most common Np isotopes are $^{237}\mathrm{Np}$ and $^{239}\mathrm{Np}$ . $^{237}\mathrm{Np}$ is usually determined by alpha counting its two major alpha transitions at 4.781 and 4.762 MeV.

text: If $^{237}\mathrm{Np}$ is separated from $^{233}\mathrm{Pa}$ which has an 86.6-keV gamma ray, the $^{237}\mathrm{Np}$ 86.6-keV gamma ray can be counted directly. $^{239}\mathrm{Np}$ is determined by gamma counting its gamma transitions at 228 and 278 keV. $^{10}$

A study on heavy water solutions of neptunium salts in which numerous well-defined bands were found in the region 1.2 to 1.8 μm is reported by Waggener.

A method with arsenazo III [1, 8-dihydroxynaphthalene-3, 6-disulfonic acid-2, 7-bis(azo-I) benzene -2-arsonic acid] is reported. $^{301-303}$ The intensity of the s:able green complex of Np(IV) and arsenazo III is measured at: $665\ \mu\text{l}$ .
The color forms instantaneously, and its intensity is constant over the concentration range 4 to 6M HNO $_3$ .
The molar extinction coefficient at $665\ \mu\text{l}$ is $\sim 10^5$ .
Chudinov and Yakovlev separated U(IV), Pu(IV), and Th(IV), which form colored complexes, from NpO $_2^{+}$ by extraction with di-2-ethylhexylphosphoric acid (HDEHP) in carbon tetrachloride.
The lower limit of detection is $0.04\ \mu\text{l}/\text{ml}$ , and the relative accuracy is between 1 and $7\%$ .
Bryan and Waterbury $^{302}$ separated Np from U and Pu by extraction with tri(iso-octyl)amine (TIOA) before determining the arsenazo III complex.
They found that of 45 elements tested, only Pd, Th, U, Pu, and Zr interfered.
The relative standard deviation was between 7.2 and $1.1\%$ in 200-mg samples of 8.5 to 330 ppm of Np.

(Procedure 20, p192.) Novikov et al. have studied the color complex of Np(IV) with Arsenazo M. $^{304}$ At 664 μ the molar extinction coefficient is $1.18 \times 10^{5}$ in 7M HCl and $9.5 \times 10^{4}$ in 0.5M HCl.
Thorin was used by several workers to analyze Np. $^{305,306}$ The absorbance is measured at 540 mu against a reference solution not containing Np.
The molar extinction coefficient measured with a Beckman DU spectrophotometer is $\sim 14,500$ .
The precision of the method at the $95\%$ confidence limits is $\pm 2.1\%$ for $0.63~\mu \mathrm{g}$ of Np/ml.
The thorin method has also been automated $^{307}$ with the Technicon AutoAnalyzer, and the precision has been improved.

Np reacts in weakly acidic solution (pH2) with xylonol orange to form a colored complex with an absorbance maximum at 550 m and molar extinction coefficient of 5.5 x $10^{4}$ .308 Xylonol orange is three times more sensitive than thorin but only one-half as sensitive as arsenazo III as a reagent for Np(IV).
Np(IV) forms a color complex with quercetin that is stable in the pH range 3 to 7 and obeys Beer's Law over a concentration range 0.4 to $4.0~\mu \mathrm{g / ml}$ .
The molar extinction coefficient is $\sim 2.3 \times 10^{6}$ at $425~\mathrm{mU}$ .
This method requires an extensive purification to remove cations and traces of organic impurities.[309]

The color complex of Np(V) and arsenazo III exists over a narrow pH range and was studied by Chudinov and Yakovlev.310 A method with the green-colored complex of Np(V) and chlorophosphonazo III was also developed by these workers.31 Chemical separation or masking agents are required for a number of elements.

Chudinov has studied spectrophotometrically the interaction of 12 organic dyes of different types with $\mathsf{NpO_2}^+$ .312 He concluded that the most promising reagents for pentavalent neptunium are 1-(2-pyridylazo) resorcinol (PAR), arsenazo III, and chlorophosphonazo III.
With PAR, the molar extinction coefficient is $\sim 4.3 \times 10^{6}$ at $510\mathrm{mu}$ .

Alpha counting is generally superior to coulometry for determining less than a few micrograms of Np; however, controlled potential coulometry is an accurate and precise method for determining Np above the 10-μg level.[313] The electrolysis cell contains a platinum electrode at which the Np(V/VI) couple is titrated in $0.5\mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{SO}_4$ .
The standard deviation ranged from $6\%$ for $7\mu \mathrm{g}$ to $0.05\%$ for about $1\mathrm{mg}$ of Np.
As little as $2\mu \mathrm{g}$ was detected.
Stromatt's method was used successfully with a gold electrode instead of a platinum electrode to eliminate the Pt/Pt(OH) $_2$ reaction reported by Fulda. $^{314}$
Fulda obtained a precision of $\pm 0.6\%$ for $6\mathrm{mg}$ of Np.
In another application of Stromatt's method, the precision was $0.1\%$ ( $n = 8$ ) for $9\mathrm{mg}$ of Np.
Also, Buckingham demonstrated a coefficient of variation for the coulometric method of $\pm 0.8\%$ for neptunium oxide and $\pm 1.1\%$ ( $n = 11$ ) for neptunium nitrate solutions.
Delvin and Duncan $^{316}$ used the same method in the presence of nitrate with $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{SO}_4$ electrolyte.
Plock and Polkinghorne $^{317}$ used coulometry to analyze solutions of dissolved alloys for both U and Np without prior chemical separation.
Propst $^{318}$ has reported a coulomteric method for Np using a conducting glass electrode in a thin-layer electrochemical cell.

This method applies to the (IV), (V), and (VI) states and was reported by Stromatt. $^{319}$ The analysis depends on the fact that $\mathsf{Np}(\mathsf{IV})$ is not oxidized, nor is $\mathsf{Np}(\mathsf{V})$ reduced at a significant rate at a platinum electrode in $\mathsf{IMH_2SO_4}$ electrolyte.

The procedure is: (1) a potential is applied at the controlled potential electrode to reduce $\mathsf{Np}(\mathsf{VI})$ to $\mathsf{Np}(\mathsf{V})$ ; (2) $\mathsf{Np}(\mathsf{V})$ is electrolytically oxidized to $\mathsf{Np}(\mathsf{VI})$ ; (3) $\mathsf{Ce}(\mathsf{IV})$ is added to oxidize $\mathsf{Np}(\mathsf{IV})$ to $\mathsf{Np}(\mathsf{VI})$ ; (4) excess $\mathsf{Ce}(\mathsf{IV})$ and $\mathsf{Np}(\mathsf{VI})$ are electrolytically reduced to $\mathsf{Ce}(\mathsf{III})$ and $\mathsf{Np}(\mathsf{V})$ ; and (5) $\mathsf{Np}(\mathsf{V})$ is electrolytically oxidized to $\mathsf{Np}(\mathsf{VI})$ . $\mathsf{Np}(\mathsf{VI})$ is determined in the first step.

The difference between the integrated currents for the first and second titrations corresponds to $\mathsf{Np}(\mathsf{V})$ , and the difference between the integrated currents for Step 5 and Step 2 corresponds to $\mathsf{Np}(\mathsf{IV})$ .

The potential for the Np(V)/(VI) couple was measured in sulfuric, perchloric, and nitric acids with a platinum electrode.[92] The potential in HCl was not obtained because of reduction of Np(VI) by chloride.
Slee and others<sup>320</sup> have reported an analytical method with square-wave polarography to determine Np in Pu after preliminary separation from Pu by TTA extraction.
Np is back-extracted from the TTA into 10M HNO<sub>3</sub>.
After several evaporation with nitric acid and wet ashing with mixed nitric and perchloric acid (to oxidize organic material), the final perchlorate residue is taken up in 1 ml of HCI.
2 ml of SM hydroxylamine hydrochloride is added, and the solution is evaporated to reduce the volume to 0.5 ml.
Then, 0.5 ml of IM EDTA is added; the solution is adjusted to pH 5.5 to 6.5 with several drops of ammonia and diluted to 5 ml.
Exactly 2 ml of the dilution are transferred to a polarographic cell and sparged with nitrogen.
The Np peak is recorded at about -0.8V against the mercury pool electrode on the square-wave polarograph.

The potential for the reduction of the EDTA complex is about 700 mV more negative than for the reduction of neptunium ions; therefore, the peak is shifted well away from the anodic oxidation of chloride ions and is well defined.
The authors report precision of ±2% and ±10% for concentration ranges of 500 and 25 ppm, respectively.
The method was used to analyze solutions with a Np content >20 mg/l containing Pu, U, Fe, Ni, Cr, and fission products, with a precision of ∼21.

Polarography and other methods were used to compare the chemistry of U, Np, and Pu by Kraus, et al.321 Jenkins used a manual polarograph to study the nature and stability of EDTA complexes with Np(III) and Np(IV).322 Np(III) and (IV) were studied in chloride and perchlorate media by Hindman and Kritchevsky.323
Np was included in a review of electrochemistry of the actinides by Milner.[126] The sulfate complexes of Np(IV) were studied polarographically by Musikas.[17]

10- to 100-mg quantities of Np were determined by redox titration. $^{22}$ $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{C}}(\mathrm{IV})$ is used to titrate Np(V) to Np(VI), and iron(II) perchlorate was used to titrate Np(VI) to Np(V).
Relative standard deviations for a single determination were $0.15\%$ for ferrous titers and $0.59\%$ for ceric titers.
Np(IV) is titrated with a solution of EDTA at pH 1.3 to 2.0 with xylenol orange as indicator.

Determination of $^{237}\mathrm{p}$ by Gamma Ray Spectrometry W.

The method is unaffected by highly salted, corrosive, or organic media. $^{237}\mathrm{Np}$ is determined by gamma spectrometry using a thin lithium-drifted germanium [Ge(Li)] semiconductor detector to resolve the 86.6-keV $^{237}\mathrm{Np}$ gamma ray.
The detector system consists of an Ortec model 3115-10200, low-energy photon detector with cooled FET preamp and a resolution of 900-keV full width at half maximum (FWHM) at 86.6-keV.
A 4096-channel pulse height analyzer is used for the accumulation and storage of the gamma spectra.
Data reduction is performed using an IBM 360 system and FORTRAN programs developed to calculate nuclide abundances from multi-channel gamma ray spectra.

Calibration. $^{233}\mathrm{Pa}$ , the daughter of $^{237}\mathrm{Np}$ , has a 0.017 abundant gamma ray at 86.6-keV that interferes with the measurement of the $^{237}\mathrm{Np}$ gamma ray of the same energy. $^{233}\mathrm{Pa}$ also has a 0.34 abundant gamma ray at 311.9-keV that is not associated with a $^{237}\mathrm{Np}$ gamma ray.
In order to determine the $^{233}\mathrm{Pa}$ contribution

to the 86.6-keV $^{237}\mathrm{Np}$ photopeak, a pure standard solution of $^{233}\mathrm{Pa}$ is required. $^{233}\mathrm{Pa}$ is purified by passing an equilibrium solution of $^{233}\mathrm{Pa}-^{237}\mathrm{Np}$ through a diatomaceous earth column which sorbs $^{233}\mathrm{Pa}$ .
The $^{233}\mathrm{Pa}$ is eluted, and the $^{2:7}\mathrm{Np}$ contaminant level is determined by alpha counting ( $^{233}\mathrm{Pa}$ does not decay by alpha emission).
Aliquots of $^{233}\mathrm{Pa}$ are pipetted into a standard gamma counting geometry.
A series of counts of the $^{233}\mathrm{Pa}$ are made to determine the ratio of gamma counts at the 86.6-keV peak to counts at the 311.9-keV peak.
The ratio is used to calculate the $^{233}\mathrm{Pa}$ contribution to the total counts detected at the 86.6-keV peak.
The standards and sample are counted in a holder which ensures a reproducible geometry at 2 cm.
